audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning a friend to get information about a job agency.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, William.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: This is Amber.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: You said to phone if I wanted to get more information about the job agency you mentioned.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Is now a good time?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Uh. Hi, Amber.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Yes, fine.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: So the agency I was talking about is called Bankside.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: They're based in Docklands.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: I can tell you the address now, 497 Eastside.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: So is there anyone in particular I should speak to there?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: The agent I always deal with is called Becky Jamieson.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Let me write that down.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Becky...
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Jamieson.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: JAMIESON.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Do you have her direct line?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Yes, it's in my contacts somewhere.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Right, here we are.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: I wouldn't call her until the afternoon, if I were you.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: She's always really busy in the morning, trying to fill last-minute vacancies.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: She's really helpful and friendly, so I'm sure it would be worth getting in touch with her for an informal chat.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: It's mainly clerical and admin jobs they deal with, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: I know you're hoping to find a full-time job in the media eventually, but Becky mostly recruits temporary staff for the finance sector.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Which will look good on your Cv, and generally pays better too.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'm just a bit worried because I don't have much office experience.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: I wouldn't worry.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: They'll probably start you as a receptionist or something like that.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: So what's important for that kind of job isn't so much having business skills or knowing lots of different computer systems.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: It's communication that really matters, so you'd be fine there.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: And you'll pick up office skills really quickly on the job.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: It's not that complicated.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: OK, good.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: So, how long do people generally need temporary staff for?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: It would be great if I could get something lasting at least a month.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: That shouldn't be too difficult.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: But you're more likely to be offered something for a week at first, which might get extended.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: It's unusual to be sent somewhere for just a day or two.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: I've heard the pay isn't too bad.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Better than working in a shop or a restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, definitely.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: The hourly rate is about 10 pounds, 11 if you're lucky.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: That's pretty good.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: I was only expecting to get 8 or 9 pounds an hour.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Do you want me to tell you anything about the registration process?
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Yes, please.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: I know you have to have an interview.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: The interview usually takes about an hour, and you should arrange that about a week in advance.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: I suppose I should dress smartly, if it's for office work.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: I can probably borrow a suit from Mum.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Good idea, it's better to look too smart than too casual.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Will I need to bring copies of my exam certificates or anything like that?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: No, they don't need to see those, I don't think.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: What about my passport?
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, they will ask to see that.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: I wouldn't get stressed about the interview though.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: It's just a chance for them to build relationship with you, so they can try and match you to a job which you'll like.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: So there are questions about personality that they always ask candidates.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Fairly basic ones, and they probably won't ask anything too difficult, like what your plans are for the future.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Hope not.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Anyway, there are lots of benefits to using an agency.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: For example, the interview will be useful because they'll give you feedback on your performance, so you can improve next time.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: And they'll have access to jobs which aren't advertised.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Exactly, most temporary jobs aren't advertised.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: And I expect finding a temporary job this way takes a lot less time, it's much easier than ringing up individual companies.
audioscript.speaker: AMBER

audioscript.text: Yes, indeed.
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: Well, I think I've covered...
audioscript.speaker: WILLIAM

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Bankside Recruitment Agency

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman giving a presentation about a holiday on an island in Britain that her company organizes.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: My name's Erica Matthews, and I'm the owner of Matthews Island Holidays, a company set up by my parents.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Thank you for coming to this presentation, in which I hope to interest you in what we have to offer.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: We're a small family-run company, and we believe in the importance of the personal touch.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: So we don't aim to compete with other companies on the number of customers.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: What we do is build on our many years' experience, more than almost any other rail holiday company,
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: to ensure we provide perfect holidays in a small number of destinations, which we've got to know extremely well.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: I'll start with our six-day Isle of Man holiday.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: This is a fascinating island in the Irish Sea, with Wales to the south, England to the east, Scotland to the north, and Northern Ireland to the west.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Our holiday starts in Heysham, where your tour manager will meet you.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Then you'll travel by ferry to the Isle of Man.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Some people prefer to fly from Luton instead.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: And another popular option is to go by train to Liverpool and take a ferry from there.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: You have five nights in the hotel, and the price covers five breakfasts and dinners.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: And lunch on the three days when there are organized trips.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Day 4 is free, and most people have lunch in a cafe or restaurant in Douglas.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: The price of the holiday includes the ferry to the Isle of Man, all travel on the island, the hotel, and the meals I've mentioned.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Incidentally, we try to make booking our holidays as simple and fair as possible.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: So unlike with many companies, the price is the same, whether you book 6 months in advance or at the last minute, and there's no supplement for single rooms in hotels.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: If you make a booking, then need to change the start date, for example because of illness, you're welcome to change to an alternative date or a different tour for a small administrative fee.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so what does the holiday consist of?
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Well, on day one, you'll arrive in time for a short introduction by your tour manager, followed by dinner in the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: The dining room looks out at the river, close to where it flows into the harbor, and there's usually plenty of activity going on.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: On day two, you'll take the coach to the small town of Peel,
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: on the way calling in at the Tynwald Exhibition.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: The Isle of Man isn't part of the United Kingdom, and it has its own parliament called Tynwald.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: It's claimed that this is the world's oldest parliament that's still functioning, and that it dates back to 979.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: However, the earliest surviving reference to it is from 1422, so, perhaps it isn't quite as old as it claims.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Day three, we have a trip to the mountain Snaefell.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: This begins with a leisurely ride along the promenade in Douglas in a horse-drawn tram.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Then you board an electric train, which takes you to the fishing village of Laxey.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: From there, it's an eight-kilometer ride in the Snaefell Mountain Railway to the top.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Lunch will be in the cafe, giving you spectacular views of the island.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Day 4 is free for you to explore, using the pass which we'll give you.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: So you won't have to pay for travel on local transport, or for entrance to the island's heritage sites.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Or you might just want to take it easy in Douglas, and perhaps do a little light shopping.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: The last full day, day 5, is for some people the highlight of the holiday, with a ride on the steam railway from Douglas to Port Erin.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: After some time to explore, a coach will take you to the Headland that overlooks the Calf of Man, a small island just off the coast.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: From there, you continue to Castletown,
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: which used to be the capital of the Isle of Man, and its mediaeval castle.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: And on day 6, it's back to the ferry, or the airport, if you flew to the island.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: And time to go home.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: Now, I'd like to tell you a bit more.
audioscript.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: Day three, we have a trip to the mountain Snaefell.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: This begins with a leisurely ride along the promenade in Douglas in a horse-drawn tram.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: Then you board an electric train, which takes you to the fishing village of Laxey.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: From there, it's an eight-kilometer ride in the Snaefell Mountain Railway to the top.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: Lunch will be in the cafe, giving you spectacular views of the island.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: Day 4 is free for you to explore, using the pass which we'll give you.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: So you won't have to pay for travel on local transport, or for entrance to the island's heritage sites.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: Or you might just want to take it easy in Douglas, and perhaps do a little light shopping.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: The last full day, day 5, is for some people the highlight of the holiday, with a ride on the steam railway from Douglas to Port Erin.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: After some time to explore, a coach will take you to the Headland that overlooks the Calf of Man, a small island just off the coast.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: From there, you continue to Castletown,
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: which used to be the capital of the Isle of Man, and its mediaeval castle.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: And on day 6, it's back to the ferry, or the airport, if you flew to the island.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: And time to go home.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: Now, I'd like to tell you a bit more.
utterances.speaker: ERICA MATTHEWS

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: According to the speaker, the company
options: has been in business for longer than most of its competitors.
arranges holidays to more destinations than its competitors.
has more customers than its competitors.

items.title: Where can customers meet the tour manager before travelling to the Isle of Man?
How many lunches are included in the price of the holiday?
Customers have to pay extra for

items: Hotel dining room has view of the <input>
Tynwald may have been founded in <input> not 979.
Travel along promenade in a tram;
Company provides a <input> for local transport and heritage sites.
Take the <input> railway train from Douglas to Port Erin
Free time, then coach to Castletown - former <input> has old castle.
Leave the island by ferry or plane
body.title: Timetable for Isle of Man holiday

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two psychology students discussing the effects of the order in which the children in a family are born.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Ed, how are you getting on with the reading for our presentation next week?
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Well, OK, Ruth, but there's so much of it.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: I hadn't realized birth order was such a popular area of research.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: But the stuff on birth order and personality is mostly unreliable.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: From what I've been reading, a lot of the claims about how your position in the family determines certain personality traits,
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: are just stereotypes with no robust evidence to support them.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: OK, but that's an interesting point.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: We could start by outlining what previous research has shown.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: There are studies going back over 100 years.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Yeah, so we could just run through some of the typical traits, like the consensus seems to be that oldest children are generally less well adjusted, because they never get over the arrival of a younger sibling.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Right, but on a positive note, some studies claim that they were thought to be good at nurturing.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Certainly in the past when people had large families, they would have been expected to look after the younger ones.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: There isn't such a clear picture for middle children, but one trait that a lot of the studies mention,
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: is that they are easier to get on with than older or younger siblings.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Hmm, generally eager to please and helpful, although that's certainly not accurate as far as my family goes.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: My middle brother was a nightmare, always causing fights and envious of whatever I had.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: As I said, none of this seems to relate to my own experience.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I'm the youngest in my family, and I don't recognize myself in any of the studies I've read about.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I'm supposed to have been a sociable and confident child who made friends easily.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: But I was actually terribly shy.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: That's funny.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: There have been hundreds of studies on twins, but mostly about nurture versus nature.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: There was one on personality which said that a twin is likely to be quite shy in social situations, because they always have their twin around to depend on for support.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: My cousins were like that when they were small.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: They were only interested in each other, and found it hard to engage with other kids.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: They're fine now though.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Only children have had a really bad press.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: A lot of studies have branded them as loners, who think the world revolves around them, because they've never had to fight for their parents' attention.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: That does seem a bit harsh.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: One category I hadn't considered before was children with much older siblings.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: A couple of studies mentioned that these children grow up more quickly and are expected to do basic things for themselves,
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: like getting dressed.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: I can see how that might be true, although I expect there's sometimes the exact opposite, playing the baby role and clamoring for special treatment.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What was the problem with most of these studies, do you think?
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: I think it was because in a lot of cases, data was collected from only one sibling per family.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Who rated him or herself, and his or her siblings at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Hmm, some of the old research into the relationship between birth order and academic achievement,
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: has been proved to be accurate though performances in intelligence tests decline slightly from the eldest child to his or her younger siblings.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: This has been proved in lots of recent studies.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Yes, although what many of them didn't take into consideration was family size.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: The more siblings there are, the likelier the family is to have a low socioeconomic status, which can also account for differences between siblings in academic performance.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: The oldest boy might be given more opportunities than his younger sisters for example.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: But the main reason for the marginally higher academic performance of oldest children is quite surprising, I think.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: It's not only that they benefit intellectually from extra attention at a young age, which is what I would have expected.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: It's that they benefit from being teachers for their younger siblings by verbalizing processes.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: And this gives them status and confidence, which again contributes in a small way to better performance.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: So, would you say sibling rivalry has been a useful thing for you?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I think so.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: My younger brother was incredibly annoying, and we fought a lot.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: But I think this has made me a stronger person.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: I know how to defend myself.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: We had some terrible arguments, and I would have died rather than apologize to him.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: But we had to put up with each other, and most of the time we coexisted amicably enough.
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: Yes, my situation was pretty similar, but I don't think having two older brothers made me any less selfish.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I was never prepared to let my brothers use any of my stuff.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: That's perfectly normal, whereas sometimes...
audioscript.speaker: RUTH

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What did findings of previous research claim about the personality traits a child is likely to have because of their position in the family?
textReadable: What did findings of previous research claim about the personality traits a child is likely to have because of their position in the family?

items: a child with much older siblings <input>
body.title: Position in family

items.title: What do the speakers say about the evidence relating to birth order and academic success?
options: There is conflicting evidence about whether oldest children perform best in intelligence tests.
There is little doubt that birth order has less influence on academic achievement than socio-economic status.
Some studies have neglected to include important factors such as family size.

items.title: What does Ruth think is surprising about the difference in oldest children's academic performance?
options: It is mainly thanks to their roles as teachers for their younger siblings.
The advantages they have only lead to a slightly higher level of achievement.
The extra parental attention they receive at a young age makes little difference.

body.title: Which TWO experiences of sibling rivalry do the speakers agree has been valuable for them?
options: learning to stand up for oneself
learning to be a good loser

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a student giving a presentation about a type of tree known as the eucalyptus, which is common in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today, I'm going to talk about the eucalyptus tree.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a very common tree here in Australia, where it's also sometimes called the gum tree.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First, I'm going to talk about why it's important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then I'm going to describe some problems it faces at present.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right, well, the eucalyptus tree is an important tree for lots of reasons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, it gives shelter to creatures like birds and bats.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these and other species also depend on it for food, particularly the nectar from its flowers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it supports biodiversity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's useful to us humans too, because we can kill germs with a disinfectant made from oil extracted from eucalyptus leaves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The eucalyptus grows all over Australia, and the trees can live for up to 400 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it's alarming that all across the country, numbers of eucalyptus are falling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because the trees are dying off prematurely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what are the reasons for this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One possible reason is disease.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far back as the 1970s the trees started getting a disease called Mundulla Yellows.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The trees' leaves would gradually turn yellow then the tree would die.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't until 2004 that they found the cause of the problem was lime, or calcium hydroxide to give it its proper chemical name.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which was being used in the construction of roads.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The lime was being washed away into the ground and affecting the roots of the eucalyptus trees nearby.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What it was doing was preventing the trees from sucking up the iron they needed for healthy growth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When this was injected back into the affected trees, they immediately recovered.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But this problem only affected a relatively small number of trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By 2000, huge numbers of eucalyptus were dying along Australia's East Coast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of a disease known as Bell-miner Associated Die-back.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The bell-miner is a bird, and the disease seems to be common where there are high populations of bell-miners.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Again, it's the leaves of the trees that are affected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What happens is that insects settle on the leaves and eat their way round them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Destroying them as they go, and at the same time, they secrete a solution which has sugar in it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The bell-miner birds really like this solution, and in order to get as much as possible, they keep away other creatures that might try to get it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So these birds and insects flourish at the expense of other species, and eventually so much damage is done to the leaves that the tree dies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But experts say that trees can start looking sick before any sign of bell-miner associated dieback.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, it looks as if the problem might have another explanation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One possibility is that it's to do with the huge bushfires that we have in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A theory proposed over 40 years ago by ecologist William Jackson is that the frequency of bushfires in a particular region affects the type of vegetation that grows there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If there are very frequent bushfires in a region, this encourages grass to grow afterwards, while if the bushfires are rather less frequent, this results in the growth of eucalyptus forests.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So why is this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Why do fairly frequent bushfires actually support the growth of eucalyptus?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, one reason is that the fire stops the growth of other species which would consume water needed by eucalyptus trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And there's another reason.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If these other quick-growing species of bushes and plants are allowed to proliferate, they harm the eucalyptus in another way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By affecting the composition of the soil and removing nutrients from it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So some bushfires are actually essential for the eucalyptus to survive, as long as they are not too frequent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, there's evidence that Australia's indigenous people practiced regular burning of bushland for thousands of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before the arrival of the Europeans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But since Europeans arrived on the continent, the number of bushfires has been strictly controlled.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now scientists believe that this reduced frequency of bushfires to low levels has led to what's known as dry rainforest, which seems an odd name, as usually we associate tropical rainforest with wet conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And what's special about this type of rainforest?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, unlike tropical rainforest, which is a rich ecosystem, this type of ecosystem is usually a simple one.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has very thick dense vegetation, but not much variety of species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The vegetation provides lots of shade, so one species that does find it ideal is the bell-miner bird.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which builds its nests in the undergrowth there, but again, that's not helpful for the eucalyptus tree.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: For example, it gives shelter to creatures like birds and bats.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these and other species also depend on it for food, particularly the nectar from its flowers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it supports biodiversity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's useful to us humans too, because we can kill germs with a disinfectant made from oil extracted from eucalyptus leaves.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The eucalyptus grows all over Australia, and the trees can live for up to 400 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it's alarming that all across the country, numbers of eucalyptus are falling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because the trees are dying off prematurely.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what are the reasons for this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One possible reason is disease.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As far back as the 1970s the trees started getting a disease called Mundulla Yellows.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The trees' leaves would gradually turn yellow then the tree would die.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It wasn't until 2004 that they found the cause of the problem was lime, or calcium hydroxide to give it its proper chemical name.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which was being used in the construction of roads.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The lime was being washed away into the ground and affecting the roots of the eucalyptus trees nearby.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What it was doing was preventing the trees from sucking up the iron they needed for healthy growth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When this was injected back into the affected trees, they immediately recovered.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But this problem only affected a relatively small number of trees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By 2000, huge numbers of eucalyptus were dying along Australia's East Coast.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of a disease known as Bell-miner Associated Die-back.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The bell-miner is a bird, and the disease seems to be common where there are high populations of bell-miners.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Again, it's the leaves of the trees that are affected.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What happens is that insects settle on the leaves and eat their way round them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Destroying them as they go, and at the same time, they secrete a solution which has sugar in it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The bell-miner birds really like this solution, and in order to get as much as possible, they keep away other creatures that might try to get it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So these birds and insects flourish at the expense of other species, and eventually so much damage is done to the leaves that the tree dies.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But experts say that trees can start looking sick before any sign of bell-miner associated dieback.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, it looks as if the problem might have another explanation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One possibility is that it's to do with the huge bushfires that we have in Australia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A theory proposed over 40 years ago by ecologist William Jackson is that the frequency of bushfires in a particular region affects the type of vegetation that grows there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If there are very frequent bushfires in a region, this encourages grass to grow afterwards, while if the bushfires are rather less frequent, this results in the growth of eucalyptus forests.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So why is this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Why do fairly frequent bushfires actually support the growth of eucalyptus?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, one reason is that the fire stops the growth of other species which would consume water needed by eucalyptus trees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And there's another reason.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If these other quick-growing species of bushes and plants are allowed to proliferate, they harm the eucalyptus in another way.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By affecting the composition of the soil and removing nutrients from it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So some bushfires are actually essential for the eucalyptus to survive, as long as they are not too frequent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, there's evidence that Australia's indigenous people practiced regular burning of bushland for thousands of years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before the arrival of the Europeans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But since Europeans arrived on the continent, the number of bushfires has been strictly controlled.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now scientists believe that this reduced frequency of bushfires to low levels has led to what's known as dry rainforest, which seems an odd name, as usually we associate tropical rainforest with wet conditions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And what's special about this type of rainforest?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, unlike tropical rainforest, which is a rich ecosystem, this type of ecosystem is usually a simple one.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It has very thick dense vegetation, but not much variety of species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The vegetation provides lots of shade, so one species that does find it ideal is the bell-miner bird.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which builds its nests in the undergrowth there, but again, that's not helpful for the eucalyptus tree.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
